title: Meridian Health Incident Response
summary: A regional healthcare provider detects suspicious activity and must triage, contain, and govern the response while protecting patient data.

sections.title: Overview
sections.body:
Meridian Health is a regional healthcare provider operating five clinics and a central data center. It stores electronic protected health information (ePHI) and is subject to HIPAA. The security team consists of three analysts and a SOC that uses a SIEM with feeds from firewalls, endpoint detection and response (EDR), authentication servers, and DNS.

At 02:14 the SIEM raises a high-severity alert: a finance workstation is making repeated outbound connections to an unfamiliar external domain, and EDR has flagged a suspicious process injecting into a system service.

sections.title: Alert Timeline
sections.body:
- 02:14 — SIEM correlation rule fires on beaconing-like DNS requests to a newly registered domain.
- 02:20 — EDR reports credential-dumping behavior on the finance workstation.
- 02:35 — Authentication logs show the finance user's account attempting to access file shares it has never accessed before.
- 02:50 — A second workstation in HR begins exhibiting the same outbound beaconing pattern.

No data exfiltration has been confirmed yet, but lateral movement appears to be in progress.

sections.title: Environment
sections.body:
The finance and HR workstations are on the same flat VLAN as most clinical workstations. Backups are taken nightly to an on-site NAS and weekly to an offline copy. The organization has an incident response plan and a documented chain-of-custody procedure, but it has not been exercised in 14 months.

The central file server holding ePHI is encrypted at rest and requires MFA for administrative access. Patch levels vary across endpoints; several finance machines are two months behind on updates.

sections.title: Compliance Obligations
sections.body: As a HIPAA-covered entity, Meridian must protect ePHI and, if a breach of unsecured ePHI is confirmed, follow breach-notification requirements. Leadership wants the response handled in a way that preserves evidence for a possible investigation and limits regulatory exposure, while restoring clinical operations quickly.

questions.question: Given active beaconing and apparent lateral movement, what is the most appropriate immediate incident response action after detection and analysis?

answers.text: Contain the affected hosts by isolating them from the network
Immediately reimage all workstations to eradicate the threat
Wait until morning to involve the full team
Power the machines off to stop the attack

questions.explanation: Following detection/analysis, the next NIST phase is containment. Network isolation stops lateral movement and beaconing while preserving volatile evidence. Reimaging is eradication (premature here), and powering off destroys volatile memory evidence and order-of-volatility data.

questions.objective: Use data sources to support an investigation
questions.question: Analysts need to confirm which external domain the workstations are contacting and identify other internal hosts doing the same. Which data source is most directly useful?

answers.text: DNS query logs
Physical badge-reader logs
HR payroll records
The asset purchase ledger

questions.explanation: DNS logs reveal which domains internal hosts resolve, making them ideal for identifying beaconing to a malicious domain and finding other hosts exhibiting the same pattern. Badge, payroll, and purchasing records are unrelated to network beaconing.

questions.objective: Apply security principles to secure enterprise infrastructure
questions.question: Leadership asks how to limit lateral movement like this in the future. Which architectural change most directly addresses the flat-network weakness?

answers.text: Network segmentation (e.g., separating finance, HR, and clinical systems into isolated VLANs with filtered flows)
Increasing the SIEM log retention period
Buying more endpoint licenses
Lengthening the password rotation interval

questions.explanation: The hosts share a flat VLAN, which enabled lateral movement. Segmenting the network into isolated zones with controlled, filtered flows between them contains compromise and limits how far an attacker can move. The other options do not constrain lateral movement.

questions.objective: Explain mitigation techniques used to secure the enterprise
questions.question: Several finance machines are two months behind on updates and the attacker exploited a known software flaw. Which mitigation would most directly have reduced this exposure?

answers.text: A consistent patch management program
A longer screen-saver timeout
Disabling the SIEM to reduce noise
Granting users local administrator rights

questions.explanation: Exploitation of a known flaw on out-of-date machines points to a patch-management gap. A consistent program to test and deploy updates promptly closes known vulnerabilities. Granting admin rights and disabling monitoring would increase risk.

questions.objective: Summarize elements of effective security compliance
questions.question: If the investigation confirms that unsecured ePHI was accessed by the attacker, what is Meridian's key compliance obligation under HIPAA?

answers.text: Follow breach-notification requirements for affected individuals and regulators
Delete all logs to avoid liability
Publicly disclose the attacker's identity
Ignore the event since data was encrypted at rest

questions.explanation: As a HIPAA-covered entity, a confirmed breach of unsecured ePHI triggers breach-notification obligations to affected individuals and the appropriate regulators. Destroying logs would be evidence tampering, and the file server's at-rest encryption does not negate a breach if the attacker accessed the data through a compromised, authorized session.
